metadata.subject: Career Education
metadata.description: Career-Life Education at the Grade 11 level supports students in deepening the career-life planning work begun in Grade 10. Students continue the cycle of exploring, reflecting, and adapting their plans — with an increasing focus on post-graduation readiness, financial literacy, mentorship, and connecting community experience to emerging career-life directions. This work may be delivered as a stand-alone course or integrated into Work Experience and other career-focused programming. First Peoples perspectives on holistic career-life balance and community contribution remain central.
metadata.general_description: A career education course deepening career-life planning, mentorship, community experience, financial literacy, and post-graduation preparation — building toward the Career-Life Connections capstone requirement in Grade 12.

big_ideas.statement: Career-life choices are made in a recurring cycle of planning, reflecting, adapting, and deciding.
big_ideas.elaboration: With more experiences under their belts, Grade 11 students can engage with the planning cycle more sophisticatedly — evaluating what they've learned from their explorations and refining their directions with greater self-knowledge and realistic awareness of opportunities.

big_ideas.statement: Career-life decisions are influenced by internal and external factors, including local and global trends.
big_ideas.elaboration: At Grade 11, students are well positioned to analyze the interplay between personal factors and external realities — including labour market data, post-secondary requirements, and emerging economic trends — and to use this analysis to make more informed choices.

big_ideas.statement: Cultivating networks and reciprocal relationships with family, peers, and community members can support and broaden career-life awareness and options.
big_ideas.elaboration: Grade 11 students can engage more actively in building professional and community networks — seeking out mentors, employers, and peers in fields of interest — and can begin contributing their own skills and energy in return.

big_ideas.statement: Finding balance between personal and work life promotes well-being.
big_ideas.elaboration: As students approach adulthood, the importance of developing sustainable rhythms — between work, study, relationships, and personal renewal — becomes increasingly concrete and personally relevant.

Grade 11 students are preparing to make important decisions about post-secondary learning. Understanding the wide range of learning pathways available — and the value of ongoing education throughout a career — positions them to make well-informed choices.

competencies.raw: Examine the influences of personal and public profiles on career-life opportunities; examine risks and benefits of digital footprints; examine the role of personal and employment networks in exploring opportunities.
i_can_statements: I can examine how my evolving personal profile — values, skills, experiences, and identity — shapes my career-life opportunities.
I can critically analyze my digital footprint and manage my online presence with increasing professionalism.
I can evaluate how my growing network of mentors, community connections, and professional contacts broadens my opportunities.

competencies.raw: Apply a mentor's guidance in career-life exploration; collaborate with supportive community members to explore reciprocal influences of career-life choices; communicate to highlight personal strengths, talents, accomplishments, and abilities.
i_can_statements: I can sustain and deepen a mentoring relationship, applying guidance to specific career-life decisions.
I can collaborate reciprocally with community members — offering my own skills and contributions while learning from theirs.
I can communicate my strengths, experiences, and goals effectively for employment, post-secondary, and community audiences.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate inclusive, respectful, and safe interactions in diverse career-life environments; identify challenges and opportunities and generate and apply strategies; explore and connect experiential learning with possible career-life pathways; practise effective strategies for healthy school/work/life balance.
i_can_statements: I can demonstrate professional, inclusive, and safe conduct across diverse workplace and community settings.
I can identify real challenges and opportunities in my career-life context and generate adaptive strategies.
I can connect my expanding experiential learning — work experience, volunteering, community projects — to emerging career-life pathways.
I can identify and practise strategies that support my well-being and sustainable balance across life domains.

competencies.raw: Explore and reflect on career-life roles and personal growth; develop preliminary profiles and flexible plans for career-life learning journeys.
i_can_statements: I can reflect on my growth over the past year and update my career-life profile to reflect new skills, experiences, and directions.
I can develop an increasingly detailed and realistic career-life plan with clear goals, timelines, and contingency strategies.
I can articulate my emerging career-life direction to mentors, family, and potential post-secondary institutions.

areas.name: Career-Life Development
areas.raw: Deepened mentorship, well-being, competency development, self-advocacy, workplace readiness.
topics: Sustained mentorship: deepening relationships with mentors in areas of career interest
Well-being and resilience: stress management, self-care routines, and mental health literacy
Core Competencies in action: demonstrating communication, critical thinking, and personal/social responsibility
Self-advocacy: communicating your needs and rights in school and workplace settings
Employment marketing: developing a professional resume, cover letter, and LinkedIn/portfolio profile
Workplace rights and safety: Employment Standards Act, WorkSafe BC, and young worker protections
Professionalism: workplace culture, conflict resolution, and professional communication

areas.name: Connections with Community
areas.raw: Networking, experiential learning, self-representation, citizenship, and intercultural skills.
topics: Deepening community connections: volunteering, work experience, co-op, and community projects
Social capital: understanding and building professional networks intentionally
Intercultural competence: working effectively with people from diverse backgrounds
Leadership development: taking initiative in school, work, and community contexts
Digital citizenship: responsible, ethical, and strategic online presence
First Peoples stewardship: exploring traditional resource management and community-based career paths
Employer relationships: job search strategies, networking, and informational interviews

areas.name: Career-Life Planning
areas.raw: Self-assessment, post-graduation possibilities, financial planning, labour market trends, portfolio.
topics: Deepened self-assessment: refining understanding of strengths, values, and transferable skills
Post-secondary exploration: researching specific programs, requirements, and application processes
Apprenticeship and trades pathways: ITA trades, dual credit, and school-based apprenticeship
Labour market analysis: BC regional trends, in-demand occupations, and wage data
Financial literacy: post-secondary costs, student loans, budgeting, and savings strategies
Portfolio development: curating authentic evidence of skills and achievements for Grade 12 capstone
Course planning for Grade 12: ensuring graduation requirements and post-secondary prerequisites are met
